title: Multiply with partial products (2-digit numbers)
description: Multiply 2-digit numbers by 2-digit numbers by multiplying by finding each partial product (such as tens of one number times the ones of the other number) separately. Then add all the partial products to find the total product.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use partial products to multiply $73\times 42$.**

||
-:|-: | :-
|$73$ |
|$\underline{{}\times 42}$ |
Step 1|[[☃ numeric-input 1]] | $2 \times {}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Step 2|$140$ | $2 \times 7$ tens
Step 3|$120$ | $40 \times 3$ ones
Step 4|$\underline{{}+2{,}800}$ | $40 \times 7$ tens
Step 5|[[☃ numeric-input 2]] |
hints:
Let's break up the top factor by place value.

Then we can multiply each part by $40$ and by $2$ to get parts of the product.

Finally, let's add up all of partial products to get the total product.

$\begin{align}
7\blueE{3}&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{4}2}&\\
6&\quad {2} \times {3\text{ ones}}\\
{140}&\quad {2} \times{7\text{ tens}}\\
\goldE{120}&\quad \maroonE{40} \times \blueE{3\text{ ones}}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\blueE{7}3&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{4}2}&\\
6&\quad {2} \times {3\text{ ones}}\\
{140}&\quad {2} \times{7\text{ tens}}\\
{120}&\quad {40} \times {3\text{ ones}} \\
\underline{{}+\goldE{2{,}800}}&\quad \maroonE{40} \times \blueE{7\text{ tens}}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
73&\\
\underline{{} \times 42}&\\
\purpleD{6}&\quad {2} \times {3\text{ ones}}\\
\purpleD{140}&\quad {2} \times{7\text{ tens}}\\
\purpleD{120}&\quad {40} \times {3\text{ ones}} \\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{2{,}800}}&\quad {40} \times {7\text{ tens}}\\
3{,}066
\end{align}$

Here is the completed equation using partial products:

$\begin{align}
73&\\
\underline{{} \times 42}&\\
{6}&\quad {2} \times {3\text{ ones}}\\
{140}&\quad {2} \times{7\text{ tens}}\\
{120}&\quad {40} \times {3\text{ ones}} \\
\underline{{}+{2{,}800}}&\quad {40} \times {7\text{ tens}}\\
3{,}066
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use partial products to multiply $35\times77$.**

||
-:|-: | :-
|$35$ |
|$\underline{{}\times 77}$ |
Step 1|$2{,}100$ | $70 \times 3$ tens
Step 2|$350$ |  $70 \times 5$ ones
Step 3|$210$|  $7 \times 3$ tens
Step 4|$\underline{{}+}$_[[☃ numeric-input 3]]_ | $7 \times {}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Step 5|[[☃ numeric-input 2]] |
hints:
Let's break up the top factor by place value.

Then we can multiply each part by $70$ and by $7$ to get parts of the product.

Finally, let's add up all of partial products to get the total product.

$\begin{align}
\blueE{3}5&\\
\underline{{} \times 7\maroonE{7}}&\\
2{,}100&\quad 70 \times 3\text{ tens}\\
350&\quad 70 \times 5\text{ ones}\\
\goldE{210}&\quad \maroonE{7} \times \blueE{3\text{ tens}}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
3\blueE{5}&\\
\underline{{} \times 7\maroonE{7}}&\\
2{,}100&\quad 70 \times 3\text{ tens}\\
350&\quad 70 \times 5\text{ ones}\\
210&\quad 7\times 3\text{ tens} \\
\goldE{35}&\quad \maroonE{7} \times \blueE{5\text{ ones}}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
35&\\
\underline{{} \times 77}&\\
\purpleD{2{,}100}&\quad 70\times 3\text{ tens}\\
\purpleD{350}&\quad 70 \times 5\text{ ones}\\
\purpleD{210}&\quad 7\times 3\text{ tens} \\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{35}}&\quad 7 \times 5\text{ ones}\\
2{,}695
\end{align}$

Here is the completed equation using partial products:

$\begin{align}
35&\\
\underline{{} \times 77}&\\
{2{,}100}&\quad 70\times 3\text{ tens}\\
{350}&\quad 70 \times 5\text{ ones}\\
{210}&\quad 7\times 3\text{ tens} \\
\underline{{}+{35}}&\quad 7 \times 5\text{ ones}\\
2{,}695
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use partial products to multiply $85\times 61$.**

||
-:|-: | :-
|$85$ |
|$\underline{{}\times 61}$ |
Step 1|$5$ | $1 \times 5$ ones
Step 2|[[☃ numeric-input 3]] | [[☃ dropdown 1]] ${}\times 8$ tens
Step 3|$300$ | $60 \times 5$ ones
Step 4|$\underline{{}+4{,}800}$ | $60 \times 8$ tens
Step 5|[[☃ numeric-input 2]] |
hints:
Let's break up the top factor by place value.

Then we can multiply each part by $60$ and by $1$ to get parts of the product.

Finally, let's add up all of partial products to get the total product.

$\begin{align}
8\blueE{5}&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{6}1}&\\
5&\quad{1} \times{5\text{ ones}}\\
{80}&\quad{1} \times {8\text{ tens}}\\
\goldE{300}&\quad \maroonE{60} \times \blueE{5\text{ ones}}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\blueE{8}5&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{6}1}&\\
5&\quad{1} \times{5\text{ ones}}\\
{80}&\quad{1} \times {8\text{ tens}}\\
{300}&\quad {60} \times{5\text{ ones}}\\
\underline{{}+\goldE{4{,}800}}&\quad \maroonE{60} \times \blueE{8\text{ tens}}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
85&\\
\underline{{} \times 61}&\\
\purpleD{5}&\quad{1} \times{5\text{ ones}}\\
\purpleD{80}&\quad{1} \times {8\text{ tens}}\\
\purpleD{300}&\quad {60} \times{5\text{ ones}}\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{4{,}800}}&\quad 60\times8\text{ tens}\\
5{,}185
\end{align}$

Here is the completed equation using partial products:

$\begin{align}
85&\\
\underline{{} \times 61}&\\
5&\quad{1} \times{5\text{ ones}}\\
80&\quad{1} \times {8\text{ tens}}\\
300&\quad {60} \times{5\text{ ones}}\\
\underline{{}+4{,}800}&\quad 60\times8\text{ tens}\\
5{,}185
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use partial products to multiply $28\times14$.**

||
-:|-: | :-
|$28$ |
|$\underline{{}\times 14}$ |
Step 1|$200$ | $10 \times 20$
Step 2|$80$|  $10 \times 8$
Step 3|[[☃ numeric-input 3]]| $4 \times {}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Step 4|$\underline{{}+32}$ | $4 \times 8$
Step 5|[[☃ numeric-input 2]] |
hints:
Let's break up the top factor by place value.

Then we can multiply each part by $10$ and by $4$ to get parts of the product.

Finally, let's add up all of partial products to get the total product.

$\begin{align}
\blueE{2}8&\\
\underline{{} \times 1\maroonE{4}}&\\
200&\quad 10 \times 20\\
80&\quad 10\times 8\\
\goldE{80}&\quad \maroonE{4} \times \blueE{20}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
2\blueE{8}&\\
\underline{{} \times 1\maroonE{4}}&\\
200&\quad 10 \times 20\\
80&\quad 10\times 8\\
80&\quad 4\times 20\\
\goldE{32}&\quad \maroonE{4} \times \blueE{8}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
28&\\
\underline{{} \times 14}&\\
\purpleD{200}&\quad 10\times 20\\
\purpleD{80}&\quad 10\times 8\\
\purpleD{80}&\quad 4\times 20\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{32}}&\quad 4\times 8\\
392
\end{align}$

Here is the completed equation using partial products:

$\begin{align}
28&\\
\underline{{} \times 14}&\\
{200}&\quad 10\times 20\\
{80}&\quad 10\times 8\\
{80}&\quad 4\times 20\\
\underline{{}+{32}}&\quad 4\times 8\\
392
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use partial products to multiply $39\times92$.**

||
-:|-: | :-
|$39$ |
|$\underline{{}\times 92}$ |
Step 1|$18$ | $2 \times 9$
Step 2|$60$| $2 \times 30$
Step 3|[[☃ numeric-input 3]] | $90 \times {}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Step 4|$\underline{{}+2{,}700}$ | $90 \times 30$
Step 5|[[☃ numeric-input 2]] |
hints:
Let's break up the top factor by place value.

Then we can multiply each part by $90$ and by $2$ to get parts of the product.

Finally, let's add up all of partial products to get the total product.

$\begin{align}
3\blueE{9}&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{9}2}&\\
18&\quad {2} \times {9}\\
{60}&\quad {2} \times{30}\\
\goldE{810}&\quad \maroonE{90} \times \blueE{9}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\blueE{3}9&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{9}2}&\\
18&\quad {2} \times {9}\\
{60}&\quad {2} \times{30}\\
{810}&\quad{90} \times {9}\\
\underline{{}+\goldE{2{,}700}}&\quad \maroonE{90} \times \blueE{30}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
39&\\
\underline{{} \times 92}&\\
\purpleD{18}&\quad {2} \times {9}\\
\purpleD{60}&\quad {2} \times{30}\\
\purpleD{810}&\quad{90} \times {9}\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{2{,}700}}&\quad {90} \times {30}\\
3{,}588
\end{align}$

Here is the completed equation using partial products:

$\begin{align}
39&\\
\underline{{} \times 92}&\\
18&\quad {2} \times {9}\\
{60}&\quad {2} \times{30}\\
{810}&\quad{90} \times {9}\\
\underline{{}+{2{,}700}}&\quad {90} \times {30}\\
3{,}588
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use partial products to multiply $56\times47$.**

|||
-:| -: | :-
|$56$ |
|$\underline{{}\times 47}$ |
Step 1 | $2{,}000$ | $40 \times 50$
Step 2 | [[☃ numeric-input 3]] | [[☃ dropdown 1]] ${} \times 6$
Step 3| $350$|  $7 \times 50$
Step 4| $\underline{{}+42}$ | $7 \times 6$
Step 5 | [[☃ numeric-input 2]] |
hints:
Let's break up the top factor by place value.

Then we can multiply each part by $40$ and by $7$ to get parts of the product.

Finally, let's add up all of partial products to get the total product.

$\begin{align}
\blueE{5}6&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{4}7}&\\
\tealE{2{,}000}&\quad \maroonE{40} \times \blueE{50}\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
5\blueE{6}&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{4}7}&\\
2{,}000&\quad 40 \times 50\\
\tealE{240}&\quad \maroonE{40} \times \blueE{6}\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\blueE{5}6&\\
\underline{{} \times 4\maroonE{7}}&\\
2{,}000&\quad 40 \times 50\\
240&\quad 40 \times 6\\
\tealE{350}&\quad \maroonE{7} \times \blueE{50} \\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
5\blueE{6}&\\
\underline{{} \times 4\maroonE{7}}&\\
2{,}000&\quad 40 \times 50\\
240&\quad 40 \times 6\\
350&\quad 7 \times 50\\
\tealE{42}&\quad \maroonE{7} \times \blueE{6}\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
56&\\
\underline{{} \times 47}&\\
\purpleD{2{,}000}&\quad 40\times 50\\
\purpleD{240}&\quad 40 \times 6\\
\purpleD{350}&\quad 7 \times 50\\
\underline{{}+\purpleD{42}}&\quad 7\times 6\\
2{,}632
\end{align}$

Here is the completed equation using partial products:

$\begin{align}
56&\\
\underline{{} \times 47}&\\
{2{,}000}&\quad 40\times 50\\
{240}&\quad 40 \times 6\\
{350}&\quad 7 \times 50\\
\underline{{}+{42}}&\quad 7\times 6\\
2{,}632
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Greg tried to evaluate $86\times37$ using partial products, but he made a mistake.  His work is below.

$\begin{array}{llrr}
&&86 \\
&&\underline{{}\times37} \\
&\goldE{\text{Step 1}}&\goldE{ 2{,}400}& \goldE{30\times 80}\\
&\maroonE{\text{Step 2}}&\maroonE{18}& \maroonE{3\times 6}\\
&\tealE{\text{Step 3}}&\tealE{560}& \tealE{7\times 80}\\
&\blueE{\text{Step 4}}&\underline{{}+\blueE{42}}& \blueE{7\times6}\\
&\purpleE{\text{Step 5}}&\purpleE{3{,}020}&
 \end{array}$

**Find Greg's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Partial products help us multiply larger numbers.

We can break the first factor apart by place value and multiply the parts *in any order* by the parts of the second factor. Then we add those products to get the final product.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's take a look at how Greg broke apart the numbers to multiply.

###Step $1$

$\begin{align}
\goldE{8}6&\\
\underline{{} \times \goldE{3}7}&\\
\goldE{2{,}400}&\quad \goldE{30} \times \goldE{80}\\
\end{align}$

There are $3$ tens in $37$, and there are $8$ tens in $86$.  Also,
$\goldE{30}\times\goldE{80}=\goldE{2{,}400}$, so Greg did not make a mistake in step $1$.

$\begin{align}
8\maroonE{6} \\
\underline{{}\times\maroonE{3}7} \\
{ 2{,}400}&\quad {30\times 80}\\
\maroonE{180}& \quad\maroonE{30\times 6}
 \end{align}$

Greg said the value of the $3$ in $\maroonE{3}7$ is $3\text{ ones}$, not $\maroonE{3\text{ tens}}$, so Greg made a mistake in step $2$.
Greg made a mistake in step $2$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Leah tried to evaluate $15\times58$ using partial products, but she made a mistake.  Her work is below.

$\begin{array}{llrr}
&&15 \\
&&\underline{{}\times58} \\
&\goldE{\text{Step 1}}&\goldE{500}& \goldE{50\times 10}\\
&\maroonE{\text{Step 2}}&\maroonE{250}& \maroonE{50\times 5}\\
&\tealE{\text{Step 3}}&\tealE{800}& \tealE{8\times 10}\\
&\blueE{\text{Step 4}}&\underline{{}+\blueE{40}}& \blueE{8\times5}\\
&\purpleE{\text{Step 5}}&\purpleE{1{,}590}&
 \end{array}$

**Find Leah's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Partial products help us multiply larger numbers.

We can break the first factor apart by place value and multiply the parts *in any order* by the parts of the second factor. Then we add those products to get the final product.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's take a look at how Leah broke apart the numbers to multiply.

###Step $1$

$\begin{align}
\goldE{1}5&\\
\underline{{} \times \goldE{5}8}&\\
\goldE{500}&\quad \goldE{50} \times \goldE{10}
\end{align}$

There are $5$ tens in $58$, and there is $1$ ten in $15$.  Also,
$\goldE{50}\times\goldE{10}=\goldE{500}$, so Leah did not make a mistake in step $1$.

###Step $2$

$\begin{align}
1\maroonE{5}&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE{5}8}&\\
500&\quad 50 \times 10\\
\maroonE{250}&\quad \maroonE{50} \times \maroonE{5}
\end{align}$

There are $5$ tens in $58$, and there are $5$ ones in $15$.  Also,
$\maroonE{50}\times\maroonE{5}=\maroonE{250}$, so Leah did not make a mistake in step $2$.

###Step $3$

$\begin{align}
\tealE{1}5&\\
\underline{{} \times 5\tealE{8}}&\\
500&\quad 50 \times 10 \\
250&\quad 50 \times 5 \\
\tealE{80}&\quad \tealE{8} \times \tealE{10}
\end{align}$

Leah said that $\tealE{8}\times\tealE{10}$ equals $800$, so Leah made a mistake in step $3$.
Leah made a mistake in step $3$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Matt tried to evaluate $49\times24$ using partial products, but he made a mistake.  His work is below.

$\begin{array}{llrr}
&&49 \\
&&\underline{{}\times24} \\
&\blueE{\text{Step 1}}&\blueE{36}& \blueE{4\times9}\\
&\tealE{\text{Step 2}}&\tealE{160}& \tealE{4\times 40}\\
&\maroonE{\text{Step 3}}&\maroonE{180}& \maroonE{20\times 9}\\
&\goldE{\text{Step 4}}&\underline{{}+\goldE{800}}& \goldE{20\times 40}\\
&\purpleE{\text{Step 5}}&\purpleE{1{,}076}&
 \end{array}$

**Find Matt's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Partial products help us multiply larger numbers.

We can break the first factor apart by place value and multiply the parts *in any order* by the parts of the second factor. Then we add those products to get the final product.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's take a look at how Matt broke apart the numbers to multiply.

###Step $1$

$\begin{align}
4\blueE{9}&\\
\underline{{} \times 2\blueE{4}}&\\
\blueE{36}&\quad \blueE{4} \times \blueE{9}
\end{align}$

There are $4$ ones in $24$, and there are $9$ ones in $49$.  Also,
$\blueE4\times\blueE9=\blueE{36}$, so Matt did not make a mistake in step $1$.

###Step $2$

$\begin{align}
\tealE{4}9&\\
\underline{{} \times 2\tealE{4}}&\\
{36}&\quad {4} \times {9}\\
\tealE{160}&\quad \tealE{4} \times \tealE{40}
\end{align}$

There are $4$ ones in $24$, and there are $4$ tens in $49$.  Also,
$\tealE4\times\tealE{40}=\tealE{160}$, so Matt did not make a mistake in step $2$.

###Step $3$

$\begin{align}
4\maroonE{9}&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE2{4}}&\\
{36}&\quad {4} \times {9}\\
{160}&\quad {4} \times{40} \\
{\maroonE{180}}&\quad {\maroonE{20}} \times\maroonE{9}
\end{align}$

There are $2$ tens in $24$, and there are $9$ ones in $49$.  Also,
$\maroonE{20}\times\maroonE{9}=\maroonE{180}$, so Matt did not make a mistake in step $3$.

###Step $4$

$\begin{align}
\goldE{4}{9}&\\
\underline{{} \times \goldE{2}{4}}&\\
{36}&\quad {4} \times {9}\\
{160}&\quad {4} \times{40} \\
{{180}}&\quad {20} \times{9} \\
\underline{~~~\goldE{800}}&\quad {\goldE{20}} \times\goldE{40}
\end{align}$

There are $2$ tens in $24$, and there are $4$ tens in $49$.  Also,
$\goldE{20}\times\goldE{40}=\goldE{800}$, so Matt did not make a mistake in step $4$.

###Step $5$

$\begin{align}
49&\\
\underline{{} \times24}&\\
\purpleD{36}&\quad 4 \times 9\\
\purpleD{160}&\quad 4 \times40 \\
\purpleD{180}&\quad 20 \times9 \\
+\underline{~~~\purpleD{800}}&\quad {20} \times{40} \\
\purpleD{1{,}176}
\end{align}$

Matt said that $\purpleD{36 + 160+ 180 + 800}$ equals $1{,}076$, so Matt made a mistake in step $5$.
Matt made a mistake in step $5$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nick tried to evaluate $42\times36$ using partial products, but he made a mistake.  His work is below.

$\begin{array}{llrr}
&&42 \\
&&\underline{{}\times36} \\
&\goldE{\text{Step 1}}&\goldE{ 1{,}200}& \goldE{30\times 40}\\
&\maroonE{\text{Step 2}}&\maroonE{600}& \maroonE{30\times 20}\\
&\tealE{\text{Step 3}}&\tealE{240}& \tealE{6\times 40}\\
&\blueE{\text{Step 4}}&\underline{{}+\blueE{12}}& \blueE{6\times2}\\
&\purpleE{\text{Step 5}}&\purpleE{2{,}052}&
 \end{array}$

**Find Nick's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Partial products help us multiply larger numbers.

We can break the first factor apart by place value and multiply the parts *in any order* by the parts of the second factor. Then we add those products to get the final product.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's take a look at how Nick broke apart the numbers to multiply.

###Step $1$

$\begin{align}
\goldE{4}2&\\
\underline{{} \times \goldE{3}6}&\\
\goldE{1{,}200}&\quad \goldE{30} \times \goldE{40}
\end{align}$

There are $3$ tens in $36$, and there are $4$ tens in $42$.  Also,
$\goldE{30}\times\goldE{40}=\goldE{1{,}200}$, so Nick did not make a mistake in step $1$.

###Step $2$

$\begin{align}
4\maroonE{2}&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE3{6}}&\\
{1{,}200}&\quad {30} \times{40} \\
{\maroonE{60}}&\quad {\maroonE{30}} \times\maroonE{2} \\
\end{align}$

Nick said the value of the $2$ in $4\maroonE{2}$ is $2\text{ tens}$, not $\maroonE{2\text{ ones}}$, so Nick made a mistake in step $2$.
Nick made a mistake in step $2$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jana tried to evaluate $75\times62$ using partial products, but she made a mistake.  Her work is below.

$\begin{array}{llrr}
&&75 \\
&&\underline{{}\times62} \\
&\blueE{\text{Step 1}}&\blueE{10}& \blueE{2\times5}\\
&\tealE{\text{Step 2}}&\tealE{140}& \tealE{2\times 70}\\
&\maroonE{\text{Step 3}}&\maroonE{300}& \maroonE{60\times 5}\\
&\goldE{\text{Step 4}}&\underline{{}+\goldE{ 420}}& \goldE{60\times 70}\\
&\purpleE{\text{Step 5}}&\purpleE{870}&
 \end{array}$

**Find Jana's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Partial products help us multiply larger numbers.

We can break the first factor apart by place value and multiply the parts *in any order* by the parts of the second factor. Then we add those products to get the final product.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's take a look at how Jana broke apart the numbers to multiply.

###Step $1$

$\begin{align}
7\blueE{5}&\\
\underline{{} \times 6\blueE{2}}&\\
\blueE{10}&\quad \blueE{2} \times \blueE{5}
\end{align}$

There are $2$ ones in $62$, and there are $5$ ones in $75$.  Also,
$\blueE2\times\blueE5=\blueE{10}$, so Jana did not make a mistake in step $1$.

###Step $2$

$\begin{align}
\tealE{7}5&\\
\underline{{} \times 6\tealE{2}}&\\
{10}&\quad {2} \times {5}\\
\tealE{140}&\quad \tealE{2} \times \tealE{70}
\end{align}$

There are $2$ ones in $62$, and there are $7$ tens in $75$.  Also,
$\tealE2\times\tealE{70}=\tealE{140}$, so Jana did not make a mistake in step $2$.

###Step $3$

$\begin{align}
7\maroonE{5}&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE6{2}}&\\
{10}&\quad {2} \times {5}\\
{140}&\quad {2} \times{70} \\
{\maroonE{300}}&\quad {\maroonE{60}} \times\maroonE{5}
\end{align}$

There are $6$ tens in $62$, and there are $5$ ones in $75$.  Also,
$\maroonE{60}\times\maroonE{5}=\maroonE{300}$, so Jana did not make a mistake in step $3$.

###Step $4$

$\begin{align}
\goldE{7}{5}&\\
\underline{{} \times \goldE{6}{2}}&\\
{10}&\quad {2} \times {5}\\
{140}&\quad {2} \times{70} \\
{{300}}&\quad {60} \times{5} \\
\underline{~~~\goldE{4{,}200}}&\quad {\goldE{60}} \times\goldE{70} \\
\end{align}$

Jana said that $\goldE{60}\times\goldE{70}$ equals $420$, so Jana made a make a mistake in step $4$.
Jana made a mistake in step $4$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Molly tried to evaluate $93\times51$ using partial products, but she made a mistake.  Her work is below.

$\begin{array}{llrr}
&&93 \\
&&\underline{{}\times51} \\
&\blueE{\text{Step 1}}&\blueE{3}& \blueE{1\times3}\\
&\tealE{\text{Step 2}}&\tealE{90}& \tealE{1\times 90}\\
&\maroonE{\text{Step 3}}&\maroonE{150}& \maroonE{50\times 3}\\
&\goldE{\text{Step 4}}&\underline{{}+\goldE{ 4{,}500}}& \goldE{50\times 90}\\
&\purpleE{\text{Step 5}}&\purpleE{4{,}650}&
 \end{array}$

**Find Molly's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Partial products help us multiply larger numbers.

We can break the first factor apart by place value and multiply the parts *in any order* by the parts of the second factor. Then we add those products to get the final product.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's take a look at how Molly broke apart the numbers to multiply.

###Step $1$

$\begin{align}
9\blueE{3}&\\
\underline{{} \times 5\blueE{1}}&\\
\blueE{3}&\quad \blueE{1} \times \blueE{3}
\end{align}$

There is $1$ one in $51$ and there are $3$ ones in $93$.  Also,
$\blueE1\times\blueE3=\blueE{3}$, so Molly did not make a mistake in step $1$.

###Step $2$

$\begin{align}
\tealE{9}3&\\
\underline{{} \times 5\tealE{1}}&\\
{3}&\quad {1} \times {3}\\
\tealE{90}&\quad \tealE{1} \times \tealE{90}
\end{align}$

There is $1$ one in $51$ and there are $9$ tens in $93$. Also, $\tealE1\times\tealE{90}=\tealE{90}$, so Molly did not make a mistake in step $2$.

###Step $3$

$\begin{align}
9\maroonE{3}&\\
\underline{{} \times \maroonE5{1}}&\\
{3}&\quad {1} \times {3}\\
{90}&\quad {1} \times{90} \\
{\maroonE{150}}&\quad {\maroonE{50}} \times\maroonE{3}
\end{align}$

There are $5$ tens in $51$ and there are $3$ ones in $93$.  Also, $\maroonE{50}\times\maroonE{3}=\maroonE{150}$, so Molly did not make a mistake in step $3$.

###Step $4$

$\begin{align}
\goldE{9}{3}&\\
\underline{{} \times \goldE{5}{1}}&\\
{3}&\quad {1} \times {3}\\
{90}&\quad {1} \times{90} \\
{{150}}&\quad {50} \times{3} \\
\underline{~~~\goldE{4{,}500}}&\quad {\goldE{50}} \times\goldE{90}
\end{align}$

There are $5$ tens in $51$ and there are $9$ tens in $93$. Also, $\goldE{50}\times\goldE{90}=\goldE{4{,}500}$, so Molly did not make a mistake in step $4$.

###Step $5$

$\begin{align}
93&\\
\underline{{} \times51}&\\
\purpleE3&\quad 1 \times 3\\
\purpleE{90}&\quad 1 \times90 \\
\purpleE{150}&\quad 50 \times3 \\
+\underline{~~~\purpleE{4{,}500}}&\quad {50} \times{90} \\
\purpleE{4{,}743}
\end{align}$

Molly said that $\purpleE{3 + 90+ 150 + 4{,}500}$ equals $4{,}650$, so Molly made a mistake in step $5$.
Molly made a mistake in step $5$.
